L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Brett chose to jump in conditions that were clearly beyond safe limits. Winds were strong with sustained gusts, flags were horizontal, conditions had worsened over 45 minutes of waiting, and two companions abandoned the jump with one explicitly warning him it was not worth it. The decision to launch was driven by desperation to get a final jump before departing the next day. The wind overpowered his canopy's forward penetration and drove him back into the cliff, producing the fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing the evening before the jump, the jumper misrouted the bridle around the right leg strap instead of routing it cleanly from the container to the pilot chute. The jumper was rushing through the pack job to finish early. The misrouted bridle locked against the leg strap under load, preventing canopy extraction despite a fully inflated pilot chute, resulting in a total malfunction and closed-container impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to fly wingsuit proximity lines in conditions of heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture, and low-level clouds — complete flat light. These conditions were observable before the jump and predictably eliminated the depth cues essential for terrain-proximity wingsuit flight. The decision to proceed placed the jumper in a situation where visual altitude reference was effectively impossible.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Visual illusion
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Flat light from featureless snow cover, absence of shadows, and low clouds eliminated all depth cues, causing the jumper to misperceive his altitude above the snowfield. Helmet camera footage confirms a relaxed body posture with no deployment initiation and no corrective input, consistent with a jumper who believed he had ample altitude. He flew into terrain at full speed without any indication he perceived the ground approaching.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump in 20-knot sustained winds with gusts well over 30 knots from the side—conditions that four experienced companions assessed as unsafe and declined to jump in. The domain reference indicates no objects are good above 20 mph (~17 knots); these conditions far exceeded acceptable limits. This decision placed him in conditions where the crosswind gust immediately destabilized his exit and pushed him laterally back toward the wall, directly producing the fatal impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wrong procedure applied
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the 3–4 seconds of tumbling along the cliff face, the jumper failed to prioritize deploying his parachute over recovering stability, and never got a hand to his pilot chute before impact. Accepted practice establishes a clear priority order: (1) deploy, (2) deploy at the right altitude, (3) deploy in a stable body position—'whatever happens, pull.' By attempting to regain stability rather than immediately initiating deployment from any attitude, he forfeited the only remaining chance of canopy extraction before striking the ledge 80 meters below.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump while severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before, despite obvious signs of impairment (vomiting twice on the approach, splitting headache, visible unsteadiness) and explicit warnings from companions to sit out. This decision placed him in a state where his physical coordination was degraded, directly contributing to the failed exit and his inability to manage the resulting emergency.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During approximately 6 seconds of freefall after going unstable on exit, the jumper fixated entirely on recovering a belly-to-earth body position and failed to deploy his pilot chute, which remained in the BOC at impact. The accepted priority order for a poor exit is to deploy regardless of body position. He had sufficient time to pitch the PC even from an unstable attitude, but task fixation on stability recovery caused him to lose altitude awareness and never initiate deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to BASE jump an intermediate-to-advanced cliff exit requiring mandatory tracking with a tracking suit in which he had completed only 2 skydives, far below the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before any BASE use. He rejected advice from a more experienced jumper to select a more forgiving exit, basing his confidence on video footage rather than personal proficiency. This decision placed him in a situation where his inability to fly the suit would be fatal.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper could not achieve effective forward drive in the tracking suit after exit. The narrative explicitly states the suit was flying him rather than the other way around, and he fell at a steep angle without generating the horizontal separation needed to clear the terrain feature where the wall comes back in. He impacted a ledge at approximately 7 seconds, never having achieved adequate separation from the cliff.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Wrong procedure applied
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Facing a 180° off-heading directly into the building on a static line jump, the jumper went straight for her toggles instead of rear risers. The domain reference specifically prescribes pulling both rear risers and landing the stalled canopy at the object's base for a static line 180° off-heading pointed directly at the object. Rear risers would have arrested forward speed rather than producing a diving turn, and risers are a larger target area, reducing the risk of grabbing only one control input.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Attempting a heading correction with brakes, the jumper found only the right toggle and pulled it hard. A proper brake correction for a 180° requires grabbing both toggles simultaneously to arrest forward speed before releasing one side. The single hard toggle input created a diving turn that drove her directly into the building's glass facade, converting an off-heading into a fatal object strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to exit a terminal cliff in essentially zero visibility conditions (fog obscuring the wall, fjord, and landing area) when all other jumpers recognized the conditions were unsafe and chose to hike down. He believed his familiarity with the exit would allow him to navigate by feel and track on the correct bearing to clear terrain unseen. This decision placed him in freefall with no ability to see terrain features, directly producing the fatal impact with a protruding ledge approximately 120 meters below the exit that he could not have seen. The pilot chute remained stowed, indicating he struck the ledge before reaching his planned deployment point.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during active tracking flight near a cliff face. This deliberate action disrupted his tracking body position, causing him to go flat and drift laterally toward the wall. Adjusting a camera mid-flight while tracking near terrain was an inappropriate prioritization that directly produced the loss of separation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: While focused on the camera adjustment, the jumper failed to monitor terrain proximity and made no corrective input despite a ledge being in clear view for several seconds before impact. He neither resumed tracking, attempted to turn away from the wall, nor deployed his parachute. His attention was captured by the camera task to the exclusion of the critical tasks of object separation and deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper continued flying wingsuit BASE from cliffs every weekend despite a known, self-acknowledged deficiency in angle of attack control that had produced at least four near-miss stalls in the preceding three months. He explicitly identified that he needed to return to the dropzone for approximately 50 skydives of dedicated stall-recovery training before further cliff flights, yet never acted on this self-assessment. This decision to repeatedly expose himself to proximity terrain with an unresolved, clearly dangerous skill gap directly placed him in the fatal situation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Approximately 15 seconds into the flight, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to increase beyond sustainable flight parameters, causing the wingsuit to stall. His glide path transitioned from shallow to nearly vertical. This was the same AoA-control failure pattern he had experienced on at least four prior occasions, confirming a persistent technique execution deficit rather than an isolated event. The stall eliminated his glide and available lift, converting controlled flight into a terminal-velocity descent into terrain with no altitude remaining for recovery or deployment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump a 900-foot antenna at night with only 2 prior antenna jumps, both from taller, more forgiving structures with guy wires further from the exit point. Accepted practice requires night jumps to be at objects well within the jumper's capability with prior daytime familiarity, and antennas provide only ~120° of safe heading range making them technically demanding. This decision placed him on an unfamiliar, restricted-heading object in reduced-visibility conditions that exceeded his antenna experience.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After the canopy opened with a 90-degree off-heading pointing directly at guy wires approximately 40 feet away, the jumper made absolutely no corrective input. Multiple witnesses confirmed no toggle input, no riser input, and arms remaining at his sides. Accepted practice requires immediate rear riser or brake input to correct heading, with a 180-degree riser turn achievable in approximately one second. The complete freeze response, consistent with unpracticed emergency reactions and lack of drilled muscle memory for heading corrections, was the direct mechanism of the fatal wire strike.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to jump with a pilot chute he knew was severely damaged — a ~4-inch tear in the mesh from the previous jump. He was explicitly told the PC needed to be replaced, and multiple people offered to lend him a spare. He examined the damage, dismissed the risk, and jumped anyway. This conscious decision to use critically damaged equipment was the direct producing mechanism of the total malfunction: the torn PC could not hold air, collapsed in his burble, and he impacted with a closed container.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David participated in a 3-way multi-way jump without establishing a proper separation plan, specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes. The domain reference for multi-way jumps explicitly requires choosing and rehearsing exit order, staged openings with vertical and horizontal separation, and pre-planned flight paths. Proceeding with the jump absent these critical safeguards placed David in a foreseeable collision scenario.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David tracked directly over the second jumper and deployed his pilot chute without checking his airspace, pitching directly into the second jumper's inflating canopy. The narrative explicitly states David did not check his airspace before deploying. This failure to maintain situational awareness of other jumpers' positions during both the tracking phase and the deployment moment directly produced the fatal canopy entanglement.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deliberately chose to deploy at a dangerously low altitude, continuing a documented pattern of progressively extending delays from 4 seconds to 6, 8, and beyond over the preceding season. The narrative explicitly states he chose to pull low and that multiple other jumpers had warned him about this behavior. On this jump the delay exceeded the available altitude, and the canopy was still extracting from the deployment bag at impact. No equipment, weather, or exit instability factors were present; the fatal outcome was produced entirely by the decision to delay deployment beyond the altitude required for canopy inflation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper chose to BASE jump an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only approximately 10 skydives in it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives in the specific suit before wingsuit BASE jumping. The domain reference explicitly states that upgrading wingsuits before fully utilizing current suit performance is a recognized error, and that even an entry-level suit requires 30-80 BASE jumps to reach full potential before considering an upgrade. This decision placed the jumper in a flight regime whose stall boundaries he did not understand, directly producing the conditions that killed him.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the second half of the flight, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to shift into a stall, causing his glide to transition from shallow to nearly vertical over approximately two seconds. He was unable to recover from the developing stall and continued sinking until terrain impact. This in-flight execution failure—failing to maintain adequate airspeed and proper AoA management, and failing to recover from the stall—was the immediate mechanism of the fatal impact, driven by insufficient familiarity with the expert suit's narrow margin between efficient flight and deep stall.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up cliff BASE jump wearing a tracking suit with only 5 total skydives and zero BASE jumps. Accepted practice requires a minimum of 200+ skydives, mentored progression from simple objects (spans), and 40-50 tracking suit skydives before any tracking suit BASE use. Jumping a terminal/sub-terminal cliff as a first-ever BASE jump with virtually no skydiving experience represents a catastrophic departure from accepted progression norms and directly placed the jumper in a situation they could not survive.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper executed an unstable exit and failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff face during sub-terminal freefall. With only 5 skydives and no BASE experience, the jumper lacked the fundamental body position, tracking, and exit skills required for cliff jumping. The resulting insufficient object separation was the direct physical mechanism producing the fatal cliff-impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Memory failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing, the jumper placed the pilot chute inside the main container on top of the folded canopy instead of routing it to the BOC pouch. When the BOC handle was pulled in freefall it extracted cleanly, but no pilot chute was connected to the deployment system—the PC remained trapped beneath the closing flaps inside the sealed container. This produced a total malfunction with no possibility of canopy extraction, resulting in fatal ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper misrigged their equipment, which produced a malfunction that could not be resolved before impact. The narrative and structured fields confirm the cause of death as misrigging and explicitly rule out manufacturer error, indicating the jumper made an error in equipment assembly or configuration. The exact nature of the misrigging is not specified, but it was the producing mechanism of the fatal malfunction.
